results.possibleIdentity: Lloyd B. Kreuzer is an American physicist and engineer who was active at Bell Telephone Laboratories during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He earned his Ph.D. from Princeton University in 1966. Kreuzer is well-known in the physics community for the 'Kreuzer Experiment' (1966), which used a Cavendish torsion balance and a sphere in a liquid of identical weight density to test the equivalence of active and passive gravitational mass to a high degree of precision (1 part in 20,000). At Bell Labs, he collaborated with C. Kumar N. Patel to develop pioneering laser-based techniques for air pollution detection, specifically using tunable infrared lasers and optoacoustic spectroscopy to measure toxic nitrogen oxide gases in concentrations as low as ten parts per billion. Kreuzer was also a participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement; he collaborated with artist Robert Wiegand for the 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum. In his later career, he was associated with Hewlett-Packard and founded Kreuzer Software, appearing on the television program 'Computer Chronicles' in the mid-1980s.
